title: Instance Methods 🏃‍♂️
description: Teach your custom objects how to perform actions.

slides.title: Verbs 🏃‍♂️
slides.content:
A Class doesn't just hold data (like `@name`), it also defines what actions the Object can perform.

We call these **Instance Methods**. They are exactly identical to normal methods, except they live inside the Class blueprint.

`my_dog.bark`

slides.title: Total Access 🔓
slides.content:
Here is the super-power of Instance Methods:

Because they technically live *inside* the Object, Instance Methods have **unrestricted access** to all of the Object's Instance Variables (data)!

You don't ever need to pass `@name` into a method as an argument, because the method already lives in the same house!

slides.title: Using Internal Data
slides.content: Run this! Notice how the `introduce` method magically has full access to the `@name` and `@age` variables established by `initialize`.
slides.codeSnippet:
class User
  def initialize(name, age)
    @name = name
    @age = age
  end
  
  def introduce
    puts "Hi! I am #{@name} and I'm #{@age} years old."
  end
end

alice = User.new("Alice", 22)
alice.introduce

quizData.question: Does an instance method need you to pass `@name` into its parameters in order to read the variable?

options.text: Yes, variable scope blocks all access.
No, instance methods have full automatic access to all `@` instance variables within their shared Object.
Only if they use the `return` keyword.

quizData.explanation: Because `@` variables and instances methods both live intimately inside the exact same Object memory, they act like family and can always see each other.

slides.title: Calling Methods ☎️
slides.content:
To trigger an instance method from the outside world (like your main script), you attach a dot `.` followed by the method name onto the specific instance variable.

`alice.introduce`

This politely asks the `alice` object to run its internal `introduce` code block.

quizData.question: If you have a `Car` instance saved in `my_honda`, how would you trigger its `honk` method?
quizData.explanation: A period `.` is the universal programming symbol for accessing an object's internal methods.
